title: Expedition to Skull Island: Pirate-themed Hex-crawling, perfect for Slow Questing
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x5fPiJUOMk0

Daniel (@dungeondive) is smitten with Expedition to Skull Island, a five-dollar print-and-play solo hex-crawl from itch.io that he calls a near-perfect chill, slow-questing experience. You captain a ship and a crew of fifteen pirates, managing skills, ammo, food, oil, medicine and discoveries as you sail to Skull Island, push inland to Skull Rock, and try to make it back to Tortuga with treasure and wonders. Skill checks roll a d6 (5–6 succeeds; a matching skill keyword adds +2), and three victory paths reward treasure, exploration, or a mix — though, as ever, the points are really just an excuse for the journey.

The heart of the game is the unexplored hex. Each new tile triggers a roll on enormous terrain-specific charts — beaches, jungle, rivers, cliffs, darkness, Skull Rock — ranging from quick skirmishes to multi-paragraph branching mini-adventures with real choices. Daniel narrates his own run with relish: sea lions granting wonder, a flood trapping him in a cave with fish-men, a stalking jungle cat that now haunts every jungle hex. He flags the rough edges honestly — inconsistent supply/medicine rules, the bookkeeping of fifteen crew, the occasional fifteen-dice roll — but insists the good shines through. A wholehearted recommendation, and a wish for more settings.

----

Expedition to Skull Island lives or dies on those huge "unexplored hex" tables full of branching surprises. What's your favourite solo game for that anything-can-happen feeling of stepping onto a brand-new tile?
